narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, predicting their chances of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 35, Year 1 school decile of 6, Year 3 decile of 2, and plans to attend full-time—and calculated an 80% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate without issues. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 academic decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled a significant drop in performance compared to your stronger Year 1 ranking of 6. Your LSAT score of 35, while solid, fell short of the dataset average of 36.7 and provided some counterbalance by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, but not enough to offset other concerns. Your upper-middle family income also contributed modestly toward the failure prediction, as the model associates certain income brackets with varying bar success patterns in the data. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students, who tend to maintain consistently higher decile rankings throughout school and stronger test scores, putting you in a riskier category despite your full-time commitment. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment highlighting where your academics dipped below the norms that correlate with bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that address your Year 3 performance dip. You might also explore part-time options or schools with more flexible admissions that weigh holistic factors beyond bar predictions. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could significantly improve your odds, as many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting their weakest areas. Keep pushing—you've already shown capability in Year 1, and with focused improvements, you can reshape this narrative.
